But if you framed it from the very beginning and you continue to tell people, the vast majority of people don't do anything, that's the deal. And so, don't expect it. So, you can be like them or you can be like the few who do. Say, I have franchise clients now and they all sweat the UFO scene, right? Because you got to start disclosing. It's easier to sell it when there's nobody. Because now you can say anything and there's no evidence. But after you got 30 of them, you got to list all their performance. In multi-level, when the FTC passed the law, I remember everybody being in trauma. Because you're going to have to disclose, and you do, the average performance, the average distributor performance per calendar year. And theoretically, it has to be disclosed to every verbal presentation and it has to be in print in every piece. I think last year's, I just looked at AMOI's new shit. It's now QuickStar. But I think their average distributor volume last year was $33.47 or something. So, you know, if I show you the marketing plan, which as you make in $50,000 a month, and then I have to say, and by the way, the average is $33.47, say everybody was in trauma about this. Well, it makes no difference if you frame it right. If you really frame it right, it actually helps. Because it gets you into choosing which of these people you want to be. Do you want to be like the mass majority who don't do shit, and here's what their average is, $33.47 a month? Or do you want to be like the few of us over here who, because of our behavior, here's what we do? And so everybody sweats this UFOC thing. God, it's going to show five winners and 25 losers. Well, yeah, of course. But let's explain that to people. And let's even use it as a positive to try and drive compliance for what we want them to do. So if you comply with these 10 things, you're probably going to be up here. If you ignore these 10 things, it's a virtual certainty you're going to be down here. That's not our problem. We give you the 10 things. It's just most people are completely dysfunctional. End of story. Well, most people know they're dysfunctional. So they like get that. But everybody sweats this all the time. So I prefer transparency. This ad I put in front of you was a famous political ad here. It's still talked about in political circles. Hardly anybody ever has the balls to run it. Carl Stokes, of course, got elected in Cleveland. And this was his main... He was black. Yeah. He was either the first or second black mayor in the United States. I think he was the first major city black mayor in the United States. And obviously the word Negro kind of tells you the time. And it was a time when they were a minority in the Cleveland population. Today, Cleveland core is like 70% black, 30% white. So it was the white lights to the summer. Yeah. So him running for mayor was a big deal. You had Clue-Cluck's Klan marching and people burning crosses and the whole routine. It has like Obama is going to have in South Carolina. A deeply divided state. Half of it for, half of it against. But this took some real Cajones to run this ad. But it was highly effective. It like makes you stop and think about your prejudice if in fact you are prejudiced. And again, very few people have the Cajones, you know, to do this. I tried to get Les Brown to do it on the Peter Lowe event and I couldn't get him to do it. What would you say? Don't buy from a Negro? Yeah, I would say... Well, I wouldn't say Negro, of course, today. But I would have him walk out on stage and say... And very early in the speech say there's a number of you in the room who are going to turn... Me off right now because of the color of my skin. That's really good. And if you do, you are going to lose out on... Right? Because see, he only worked. He got good numbers in cosmopolitan metropolitan areas. We could put him on in Atlanta. We couldn't put him on in Birmingham. I mean, he just flopped. And the elephant in the room was the color of his skin. So why not confront it? Make it a positive or a negative. Yeah. So I'm big on this transparency thing and I think you have to... If you look at the next page, these are from actually a current super conference brochure you'll be seeing. But like I think that you have to identify what's in their minds that are the negatives and confront them. So like the first testimonial, I was thrilled to see it and immediately wrote the headline for it. Right? Because it addresses something we're all usually afraid to address about seminars in our marketing materials, even though the vast majority of the people we're marketing to have it in their heads, that I'm going to come to this event and everybody's going to do nothing but spend all their time trying to sell me shit. Right? And this is especially true of the people who come back year after year after year after year. Because in many of the niches, you know, part of our new problem is we've been so good at pressing price point that we have a guy who's been to three events and he's got a shelf of stuff above his head. He used to be notebooks he paid $200, $300 bucks for. Now it's notebooks he paid $3,000, $4,000, $5,000 for that he hadn't used. So the shrink wrap on 10 $5,000 notebooks that he hasn't opened yet is a bigger barrier to coming and getting more notebooks than was the shrink wrap on 10 $300 notebooks. And so to ignore all of that, I believe is just foolish. So most of the copy I write about seminars has a whole bunch of copy about the reasons why you wouldn't come. But this is a good, this testimonial is like gold as far as I'm concerned. It's not quite as good as that Cummings thing Jeff Paul and I had where the guy gave blood to buy the course and slept in his car and then became a millionaire. But I mean it's good. We lived off of that for two years. And then the bottom one deals with sort of a different problem, which is the too smart for the room problem. Right? I've already heard it all. You know, why do I need to be back? Well, we got a guy we can trot out who's been coming for 23 years. Right? So now the guy who thinks he got too smart for the room in five, he's got to kind of rethink that position. But part of it is being willing to put it on the table and talk about it rather than ignore it. Right? And this obviously has sales application, but it has like bigger, broader application like, you know, retention issues. Everybody's usually afraid to talk about the fact that a lot of people leave. Right? But in reality, if you're not willing to talk about the fact that a lot of people leave, you don't get a chance to try and make you not leave. Because the best way to make you not leave is to talk about what's in your head that would cause you to leave and deal with it. So it has sales letter application, but again, it has much broader application than just sales letters. And so transparency is really about deciding what all the problems are, what all the flaws are, what all the obstacles to having the influence you want are, and then tackling them. And if you can, converting them to opportunities, as in the Carl Stokes example, rather than, you know, rather than not. Kind of back to my Peter Lothings. You see, one of the things that I identified at the very beginning about that time slot was one of its first inherent problems is, of course, everybody's leaving. But its second inherent problem is the ones who stay. This time slot has no prestige. So there's a certain preconditioning of this can't be very important or valuable if it is stuck here at the end of the day with two-thirds of the people streaming out the doors. The person staying kind of looks around and says, well, maybe I should be leaving too. Yeah, well, you can ignore that, but that's no good. So, because like it's kind of obvious, you know, it's not like you don't notice. So like after the first city. Excuse me, excuse me. Yeah, I mean, so like after the first city, I said, well, just starting with my content, I'm going to cut this, you know, now I chose to make fun of it, but I acknowledged it. And so I acknowledged the fact that like I know they're all even guys, you know, and then I chose to make fun of it and then quickly get into promises. But one way or another, I think you got to acknowledge the elephants in yourself and in your business and in your individual propositions. That's how you got me in this whole thing, just that third thing. I actually shut those when I heard you say that. All right, I'll do this guy. Oh, yeah, look, I did that here, but that's that's how I got it. Oh, no, it's not. Yeah, it's actually a common. No, that was like essential. And that whole opening, like four and a half minutes or so, you would see people at the door turning around coming back.
